At oral argument, Chief Justice Peterson pointed out to Leslie that the trial court’s order denying the defendant’s motion for a new trial, which was based on the prosecution’s proposed order, contained at least five citations to nonexistent cases, at least five citations to cases that didn’t support the proposition for which they were cited, and three nonexistent quotations.

What did Leslie have to say for herself? She acknowledged preparing a draft order, but said it was revised by the court prior to issuance—i.e., she threw the trial judge, Judge Jewel Scott, under the bus. But Chief Justice Peterson, who had clearly done his homework, immediately pointed out that “[t]hose nonexistent cases were cited in your initial brief opposing the motion for a new trial.” After rifling through her papers at the lectern, Leslie said, “I’m not aware of that, but I would be glad to research and provide the court with a supplement.”

Don’t worry about it, Ms. Leslie; Court TV did your work for you (much like an AI tool). After comparing the 37-page proposed order prepared by the state with the 33-page order ultimately issued by Judge Scott, Court TV found that the citations lined up. So Leslie can’t get out of this pickle by blaming the judge (although as Professor Carissa Byrne Hessick pointed out, Judge Scott isn’t blameless here either, since she apparently adopted the prosecution’s citations without verifying them independently).

In an age where you can use technological tools to catch hallucinated cases, AI fails are increasingly difficult to defend. But they will continue to happen, because AI assistants are not infallible. So if you make such a mistake, don’t deny or deflect; instead, acknowledge it openly and apologize (as did this Biglaw partner—and as did I, after using an embarrassing AI-generated image that I didn’t bother to review).

Other lawyers in the news:

If you need something from the Trump administration, your first phone call should be to Mike Davis. He claims to be “the best fixer in Washington, period”—and based on the reporting of The Wall Street Journal (gift link), he seems to have the track record to back this claim up.

In the wake of the many lawyers who have quit their jobs with the federal government (or have been fired), the Justice Department needs more than a few good men (and women). So the DOJ is waiving its longstanding policy requiring newly hired federal prosecutors to have at least one year of experience.

Speaking of lawyers leaving the Trump administration, Margaret “Meg” Ryan resigned as director of the enforcement division of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). She stepped down after only six months in the position, without giving a reason; if you have info as to why, feel free to drop me a line.

In memoriam:

Robert S. Mueller III—who served as director of the FBI for 12 years, then later as the special counsel who investigated Russian interference in the 2016 presidential election—passed away at 81. He had a long and distinguished legal career that included, in addition to his most high-profile roles, service as a federal prosecutor in San Francisco and Washington, D.C.; leadership of the Criminal Division at the DOJ; and multiple stints in private practice at WilmerHale. I had the privilege and pleasure of meeting Bob Mueller, at a dinner in 2014, and came away impressed. (For a photo of me holding forth while Mueller listened intently—or at least pretended to)—see this LinkedIn post by Bob Ambrogi.)

John J. Park, Jr.—who spent much of his legal career in public and government service, including with the U.S. Army JAG Corps and the Alabama Attorney General’s Office—passed awayat 71.

May they rest in peace.

Judge of the Week: Chief Justice John Roberts.

Is Chief Justice John Roberts like Obi-Wan Kenobi? Does he grow more powerful the more you strike him down?

Adam Liptak—writing in The Docket, his legal newsletter for The New York Times (gift link)—highlighted a Truth Social diatribe that Donald Trump issued against the Court last Sunday. It amounted to an extended diatribe about the justices for voting against Trump’s interests in the 2020 election and again in the recent tariffs case—but in Liptak’s view, it amounted to “a gift” to the Chief and his fellow justices, since it “inadvertently made the case for the Court’s independence.”

In terms of his official title, Chief Justice Roberts is actually the chief justice not of the Supreme Court, but of the United States. As the leader of the entire federal judiciary, his job includes protecting judges from unwarranted hostility and threats—which are sadly on the rise, as five lower-court judges discussed at a virtual event.

During a Tuesday event at Rice University, Judge Lee Rosenthal (S.D. Tex.) asked Chief Justice Roberts how he deals with criticism. After noting that it comes with the job if you’re a judge, he added that sometimes “the criticism can move from the focus on legal analysis to personalities.” Such “personally directed hostility,” according to the Chief, “is dangerous—and it’s got to stop.” Chief Justice Roberts’s remarks generated extensive media coverage, collected at How Appealing by Howard Bashman (along with video—the key discussion begins around the 23-minute mark).

The Supreme Court has received criticism for not being sufficiently supportive of lower-court judges, especially when they rule against the Trump administration. So I think the Chief’s remarks—which didn’t call out Trump by name, but clearly implicate him and his supporters—are noteworthy. As Judge Rosenthal put it, “On behalf of trial judges everywhere, I want to personally thank you because while we know that you may not always agree with us, we always know that you have our backs, and that means a great deal. So, thank you—and I hope it continues.”

In other news about judges and the judiciary:

Sorry, he’s not sorry: in a speech delivered after his controversial “swinging dicks” dissent, Judge Lawrence VanDyke argued that his Ninth Circuit colleagues are the ones who should apologize, for “routinely impos[ing] their personal policy preferences on the law.” He then offered them a deal: “You stop breaking the norm of how our system was designed to work, and I’ll stop breaking those norms that promote collegiality and the perception of legitimacy.” For anyone who isn’t tired of this story—i.e., can’t get enough “dick,” à la Blanche Devereaux of The Golden Girls—check out Sarah Isgur and David French’s discussion on Advisory Opinions. (And for anyone who skimmed my story about that dissent too quickly, please note that this sentence was a joke: “Not to be outdone, Judge James Ho subsequently issued a Fifth Circuit opinion that opened as follows: ‘This is a case about huge, throbbing cocks.’”)

Judge Zahid Quraishi (D.N.J.) is quite upset over the leadership structure at my former workplace, the U.S. Attorney’s Office in New Jersey, currently being run by a “triumvirate” after the departure of Alina Habba—and he took it out on two assistant U.S. attorneys, Daniel Rosenblum and Mark Coyne, who appeared before him for a sentencing. After reading the transcript, my own view is that Judge Quraishi was unnecessarily harsh toward the two AUSAs, who didn’t come up with this (admittedly bizarre) scheme but are stuck defending it. (Disclosure: I’m not unbiased, since Mark Coyne is a friend and former colleague of mine.)

In nominations news, the Senate confirmed Anna St. John to serve as a judge on the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Louisiana, by a vote of 51-45. St. John currently serves as president and general counsel of the Hamilton Lincoln Law Institute, a nonprofit public-interest firm (mentioned in last week’s Judicial Notice for filing a proposed class action on behalf of a putative class of drivers who got stuck for hours in a massive traffic jam caused by a pro-Palestinian protest).

In memoriam: Judge E. Grady Jolly, who served on the Fifth Circuit for more than 40 years, passed away at 88. For personal reflections on Judge Jolly from a former clerk, see Ilya Shapiro’s tribute over at City Journal.

Job of the Week: an opportunity for an appellate litigator in Houston.

Lateral Link is working exclusively with the leader of a Chambers-ranked appellate group in Houston seeking a strong associate to join its team. This is a rare opportunity to join a highly respected, close-knit practice where associates take on meaningful responsibility early and work directly with partners on complex, high-level matters. The group is known for its collegial, mentorship-driven culture, offering a clear path to partnership. They seek an associate with two to seven years of experience, a federal trial- or appellate-court clerkship, outstanding academic credentials, and excellent research and writing skills. If you’re interested—or even just curious—please contact Wendy Boone at wendyboone@laterallink.com.

Rulings of the Week: Ford v. McKesson and United States v. Burger.

Last week, I highlighted a trio of Ninth Circuit rulings in First Amendment cases that generally came down on the side of free speech (and please note the update I appended to my discussion of NetChoice, LLC v. Bonta). But this week, I’m featuring a pair of Fifth Circuit rulings, issued on the same day, in which the First Amendment claims did not prevail:

Ford v. McKesson. In this long-running litigation filed by John Ford, a Baton Rouge police officer, against DeRay McKesson, a Black Lives Matter activist, a divided panel of the Fifth Circuit ruled that after eight years of legal sparring, “[i]t is time for Officer Ford to have a jury assess his claim that DeRay Mckesson’s negligence in leading a violent protest caused him to suffer injuries at the hands of rioters.” Judge Edith Jones wrote the majority opinion; Judge Carolyn Dineen King dissented, arguing that the decision “imperils First Amendment liberties.”

United States v. Burger. In January 2025, a federal grand jury indicted James Wesley Burger, an 18-year-old high school senior at the time, on three counts of transmitting threats in interstate commerce. But he didn’t make these alleged threats in person or over the phone; instead, he made them to other users of the game Roblox. The district court dismissed the indictment, concluding that no reasonable juror could find that Burger’s statements were “true threats” outside the protection of the First Amendment. But in a unanimous, per curiam opinion, the Fifth Circuit reversed, holding that “a trial on the merits is necessary to evaluate the sufficiency of the government’s evidence.”

The Fifth Circuit is the most conservative circuit court in the country, and this sometimes manifests itself in decisions that could be construed as against free speech (although note that some of the court’s conservative members, including Judges Jim Ho and Don Willett, are some of the strongest defenders of the First Amendment). I also think it’s fair to say that Ford and Burger might have come out differently if they had been litigated in another, more liberal circuit court.

But note a common theme tying together Ford and Burger: they could be viewed less as cases against free speech and more as cases in favor of letting juries decide. In these two litigations, context matters—the context of the protest, the context of the video game—and jurors might be better situated than judges to make the required calls. So let’s see how the trials turn out before fearing for the First Amendment.

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

State of New York v. Trump. From John Ross of Short Circuit: “Within days of President Trump beginning his second term, OMB orders a sweeping freeze to all federal financial assistance programs. The following day, 22 states sue, alleging that the freeze is unlawful. The following day, OMB rescinds the memo announcing the policy. Feds: So the lawsuit is moot. First Circuit [by Chief Judge David Barron]: Then why did the White House Press Secretary post, ‘This is NOT a rescission of the federal funding freeze. It is simply a rescission of the OMB memo’? The case is live and the preliminary injunction against the freeze is affirmed. But the part of the injunction actually requiring disbursement of the money is vacated.”

In terms of Trump-related litigation in the district courts:

Judge Brian Murphy (D. Mass.) blocked the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) from implementing certain vaccine-related policies promulgated by Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.

Speaking of Secretary Kennedy, Judge Mustafa Kasubhai (D. Or.) blocked HHS’s effort to restrict healthcare providers from providing gender-affirming care to minors.

Judge Paul L. Friedman (D.D.C.) held unconstitutional the Trump administration’s new restrictions on media reporters and news organizations covering the Pentagon—and noted that “in light of the country’s recent incursion into Venezuela and its ongoing war with Iran, it is more important than ever that the public have access to information from a variety of perspectives about what its government is doing.”

Staying on the media beat, Judge Royce Lamberth (D.D.C.) ruled that the administration’s near shutdown of Voice of America was illegal—and ordered the reinstatement of more than 1,000 people who were placed on leave at the government-funded broadcaster.

On Friday, a federal jury in San Francisco (N.D. Cal.) found Elon Musk liable for some losses experienced by Twitter investors, based on comments he posted on social media threatening to walk away from his purchase of the company. Damages remain to be determined, but lawyers for the plaintiff investors claim that Musk might have to pay former Twitter shareholders up to $2.5 billion.

Whenever you see a giant jury verdict, take it with a grain of salt, at least until all post-trial motions and appeals have been resolved. Los Angeles County Superior Court Judge Ruth Kwan granted Johnson & Johnson’s motion to set aside an award of $950 million in punitive damages to the family of a woman who died from mesothelioma, which they claimed was caused by J&J’s talc products.

Turning to the state courts, my favorite verdict of last week came in Shawn D. Cooley v. John Edgar Foreman aka Afroman. In 2022, police officers in southern Ohio raided Afroman’s home, investigating (false) tips of kidnapping and drug trafficking. The police found no evidence of such crimes and criminal charges were never brought, but the officers did cause significant damage to Afroman’s property. He did what you’d expect a rapper to do: he made multiple (humorous) songs about the raid, using real video from the raid (caught on his home security cameras), and he mocked the officers mercilessly, calling them out by name. They sued him, claiming defamation and privacy violations. But they had a weak case, as explained by Aaron Terr of the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE), and a jury ruled entirely in Afroman’s favor last Wednesday. For more on the case, read this tweet, watch this video, or read this Times article.

Litigations of the Week: Noem v. Doe and Trump v. Miot.

Regular readers of Judicial Notice are aware of the numerous cases bouncing around the federal courts relating to Temporary Protected Status (TPS). The TPS program allows non-citizens from designated countries to live temporarily in the United States

The Trump administration has been trying to end TPS as to a slew of different countries, but lower-court judges have frequently ruled to prevent the government from ending TPS. As discussed last week, the federal government recently requested interim relief from the Court, asking the justices to allow it to end TPS for immigrants from Haiti, and Solicitor General John Sauer asked the Court to grant certiorari before judgment and reach the merits—to end what he called the “unsustainable cycle” of SCOTUS giving the green light to TPS terminations, followed by lower-court judges finding other grounds for preserving TPS status.

Last Monday, the Court granted cert before judgment in cases involving TPS for Syrian and Haitian nationals. The justices will hear argument in Noem v. Doe (Syria) and Trump v. Miot (Haiti) in April, with a ruling likely to follow near the end of the Court’s Term, sometime in late June or early July. For more on this incredibly important litigation—which affects hundreds of thousands of individuals and implicates important legal issues—check out Advisory Opinions.

In other litigation news:

The D.C. Circuit granted the Trump administration’s motion to withdraw its motion to voluntarily dismiss its appeals in the Biglaw executive-order cases—i.e., to keep on defending the EOs in court.

Twenty-three states and 14 cities and counties sued the administration, challenging its decision to revoke the federal government’s “endangerment finding”—its 2009 conclusion that climate change is a serious threat.

Two fired F.B.I. agents sued the FBI, FBI Director Kash Patel, the DOJ, and Attorney General Pam Bondi, challenging the legality of their firings.

The DOJ filed another lawsuit against Harvard, alleging that the university remains “deliberately indifferent” to harassment of Jewish and Israeli students.

The DOJ moved to dismiss the criminal case against two former police officers in Louisville, Kentucky, who allegedly falsified the search warrant that ultimately led to the fatal shooting of Breonna Taylor.

Former New Jersey attorney general Matt Platkin, now in private practice, told me in our recent podcast interview that he and his colleagues at Platkin LLP were working on a number of socially significant lawsuits. Earlier this month, they sued Open AI, alleging that ChatGPT is an addictive and dangerous product.

Deal of the Week: Nexstar’s $6.2 billion acquisition of Tegna.

On Thursday, after receiving approval from the Federal Communications Commission, Nexstar Media closed its $6.2 billion acquisition of a rival TV company, Tegna. The post-merger entity oversees around 265 television stations in 44 states, making it one of the nation’s biggest owners of local television stations.

The law firms on the deal are Kirkland & Ellis, Wiley Rein, and Morrison Foerster, representing Nexstar, and Wachtell Lipton and Covington Burling, counseling Tegna. And even though the transaction closed, the lawyers might still have more work ahead of them: last Friday, eight states moved for a temporary restraining order to block Nexstar and Tegna from proceeding with their integration, based on antitrust grounds—specifically, too much concentration in certain local TV markets.

Coming on the heels of dozens of states continuing to prosecute the antitrust case against LiveNation (discussed in last week’s JN), the effort to block the Nexstar/Tegna merger is another reminder that the federal government isn’t the only game in town when it comes to antitrust enforcement. But whether states can be as effective as the feds in this area remains to be seen.

Law Firm of the Week: Kirkland & Ellis.

Thanks in large part to its work advising on billion-dollar deals like Nexstar/Tegna, Kirkland & Ellis set another record. Based on its 2025 financial results, K&E became the first law firm in history to hit more than $10 billion in annual revenue—and its equity partners earned more than $11 million in profits per equity partner.

Around $3 billion of that revenue comes from the litigation side, which means that the bulk of Kirkland’s revenue comes from its transactional work. As I’ve discussed in these pages before, K&E has evolved from being a litigation-focused firm to a deal-driven one, with a particular emphasis on work for private-equity clients.

As a result of this shift, Kirkland seems to have less tolerance for litigators who might generate controversy—even if those litigators might be extremely successful partners with large practices. In 2022, for example, Paul Clement and Erin Murphy departed from K&E and launched Clement & Murphy, after their work on Second Amendment cases rubbed some of Kirkland’s corporate clients the wrong way.

The latest heavy-hitting litigator to leave Kirkland is Jay Lefkowitz, who’s departing from the firm after more than 30 years. He’s not moving to another firm or going in-house; instead, he said that he’ll be transitioning into “teaching, working with non-profit organizations. and helping some exciting young companies.”

What’s prompting Lefkowitz’s departure? Here’s one possibility: in 2007, he was part of a legal team that represented Jeffrey Epstein, obtaining what was widely viewed as a sweetheart deal for some of the late financier’s sex crimes. And the two men interacted outside the context of Lefkowitz’s legal representation: as reported by The Financial Times, Lefkowitz invited Epstein to his son’s bar mitzvah, and Lefkowitz also asked to borrow Epstein’s helicopter for a trip to the Hamptons.

When asked by Patrick Smith of The American Lawyer about whether his retirement from Kirkland might be related to his work for Epstein, Lefkowitz responded with a statement: “In 2007, I represented Epstein, like every one of my clients, as effectively as I could. Although I believe everyone is entitled to representation no matter how heinous their crimes, knowing what we all know now, I would not have taken on the matter.” As for Kirkland, a firm spokesperson stated, “To say the least, we deeply regret the firm’s 2007 representation of Jeffrey Epstein. It was clearly a mistake to take that on and, without question, we would not have made the same decision today.”

In other news of departures from K&E, partners are starting to follow David Nemecek, a leading figure in the liability-management space, over to his new home, Simpson Thacher. They include restructuring partners Brian Schartz and John Luze, as reported by The WSJ, and restructuring partner Christine Bae and finance partner Jacob Ruby, per Law.com.

Move of the Week: Manatt hiring entertainment lawyers Ellie Heisler, Jessica Boardman, and Mike Lawhead.

Since the Oscars just took place, it seems fitting to feature showbiz lawyers as the stars of the latest Move of the Week. As reported by Variety and Law360, three entertainment partners—Ellie Heisler, Jessica Boardman, and Mike Lawhead—joined the Los Angeles office of Manatt Phelps & Phillips. Heisler, Lawhead (great name for an attorney), and associate April Hua joined from Nixon Peabody; Boardman came from an entertainment-focused boutique, Myman Greenspan.

Other notable moves:

Not too far away (depending on L.A. traffic), Goodwin Procter opened an Orange County office in Newport Beach. The O.C. outpost will be led by Richard Grabowski, who left Jones Day after 36 years, along with two other litigation partners, Ryan Ball and John Vogt.

Speaking of new offices, Reed Smith launched in Boston, its fourth new outpost in 14 months, with a dozen lawyers from seven different Am Law 50 firms—including Grant Butler, a Chambers-ranked regulatory lawyer focused on the financial industry, who came from K&L Gates.

Prominent trial lawyer Hildy Sastre moved to the Miami office of DLA Piper from Shook Hardy & Bacon, where she had co-chaired the product-liability practice.

Teal Luthy Miller, former acting U.S. attorney for the Western District of Washington, joined the Seattle office of Davis Wright Tremaine. (Fun fact: she’s married to Judge Eric Miller of the Ninth Circuit, whom she met when they were classmates at the University of Chicago Law School.)

Abbe Lowell, who has been extremely busy with his many Trump-related matters, hired his first partner at Lowell & Associates: former federal prosecutor Caleb Hayes-Deats, who came from MoloLamken.

Thanks for joining me for this edition of Judicial Notice. I’m glad to be back in the swing of things, after a lovely vacation, and I wish you all the best in the week to come.

item.title: Judicial Notice (03.22.26): The $10 Billion Law Firm
item.description: This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by Burford Capital helps companies and law firms unlock the value of their legal assets. With a portfolio of over $7 billion and listings on the NYSE and LSE, Burford provides capital to finance high-value commercial litigation and arbitration—without adding…
item.author: David Lat
item.content:encoded:
This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by

Burford Capital helps companies and law firms unlock the value of their legal assets. With a portfolio of over $7 billion and listings on the NYSE and LSE, Burford provides capital to finance high-value commercial litigation and arbitration—without adding cost or risk or giving up control. Clients include Fortune 500 companies and Am Law 100 firms, who turn to Burford to pursue strong claims, manage legal costs and accelerate recoveries. Learn more at burfordcapital.com.

I wrote much of this edition of Judicial Notice while on our flight back home from spring break in Costa Rica yesterday. We had a great time; after a few days at the beach in Guanacaste, we headed to the La Fortuna and Arenal area, where we enjoyed the hot springs, did some hiking, and saw plenty of wildlife. As usual after a vacation, our main challenge now is getting back into the work and school mindset.

We did deal with some work while away. On Tuesday, I participated in the (excellent) New York City Bar Association webinar about (insanely early) Biglaw recruiting of law students. And on Friday, Zach and I hopped online for the SCOTUSblog live blog (although it wasn’t terribly exciting—with all due respect to Justice Elena Kagan, author of the Supreme Court’s unanimous opinion in Olivier v. City of Brandon).

Now, on to the news.

Lawyer of the Week: Deborah Leslie.

In recent weeks, I’ve written about a (now former) federal prosecutor who found himself in hot water after possibly using AI to draft a court filing. The same problem might have affected a state prosecutor—and the moment went viral.

Unfortunately for Deborah Leslie of the Clayton County District Attorney's Office, she got called out by Chief Justice Nels Peterson of the Georgia Supreme Court, for a filing that contained nonexistent cases. The exchange was caught on courtroom cameras and posted to X by Anna Bower—where it has, as of this writing, been viewed 5 million times.

The offending filing was a draft order denying a new trial that was filed by the prosecution and adopted by the trial court. At oral argument, Chief Justice Peterson pointed out to Leslie that the trial court’s order denying the defendant’s motion for a new trial, which was based on the prosecution’s proposed order, contained at least five citations to nonexistent cases, at least five citations to cases that didn’t support the proposition for which they were cited, and three nonexistent quotations.

What did Leslie have to say for herself? She acknowledged preparing a draft order, but said it was revised by the court prior to issuance—i.e., she threw the trial judge, Judge Jewel Scott, under the bus. But Chief Justice Peterson, who had clearly done his homework, immediately pointed out that “[t]hose nonexistent cases were cited in your initial brief opposing the motion for a new trial.” After rifling through her papers at the lectern, Leslie said, “I’m not aware of that, but I would be glad to research and provide the court with a supplement.”

Don’t worry about it, Ms. Leslie; Court TV did your work for you (much like an AI tool). After comparing the 37-page proposed order prepared by the state with the 33-page order ultimately issued by Judge Scott, Court TV found that the citations lined up. So Leslie can’t get out of this pickle by blaming the judge (although as Professor Carissa Byrne Hessick pointed out, Judge Scott isn’t blameless here either, since she apparently adopted the prosecution’s citations without verifying them independently).

In an age where you can use technological tools to catch hallucinated cases, AI fails are increasingly difficult to defend. But they will continue to happen, because AI assistants are not infallible. So if you make such a mistake, don’t deny or deflect; instead, acknowledge it openly and apologize (as did this Biglaw partner—and as did I, after using an embarrassing AI-generated image that I didn’t bother to review).

Other lawyers in the news:

If you need something from the Trump administration, your first phone call should be to Mike Davis. He claims to be “the best fixer in Washington, period”—and based on the reporting of The Wall Street Journal (gift link), he seems to have the track record to back this claim up.

In the wake of the many lawyers who have quit their jobs with the federal government (or have been fired), the Justice Department needs more than a few good men (and women). So the DOJ is waiving its longstanding policy requiring newly hired federal prosecutors to have at least one year of experience.

Speaking of lawyers leaving the Trump administration, Margaret “Meg” Ryan resigned as director of the enforcement division of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). She stepped down after only six months in the position, without giving a reason; if you have info as to why, feel free to drop me a line.

In memoriam:

Robert S. Mueller III—who served as director of the FBI for 12 years, then later as the special counsel who investigated Russian interference in the 2016 presidential election—passed away at 81. He had a long and distinguished legal career that included, in addition to his most high-profile roles, service as a federal prosecutor in San Francisco and Washington, D.C.; leadership of the Criminal Division at the DOJ; and multiple stints in private practice at WilmerHale. I had the privilege and pleasure of meeting Bob Mueller, at a dinner in 2014, and came away impressed. (For a photo of me holding forth while Mueller listened intently—or at least pretended to)—see this LinkedIn post by Bob Ambrogi.)

John J. Park, Jr.—who spent much of his legal career in public and government service, including with the U.S. Army JAG Corps and the Alabama Attorney General’s Office—passed awayat 71.

May they rest in peace.

Judge of the Week: Chief Justice John Roberts.

Is Chief Justice John Roberts like Obi-Wan Kenobi? Does he grow more powerful the more you strike him down?

Adam Liptak—writing in The Docket, his legal newsletter for The New York Times (gift link)—highlighted a Truth Social diatribe that Donald Trump issued against the Court last Sunday. It amounted to an extended diatribe about the justices for voting against Trump’s interests in the 2020 election and again in the recent tariffs case—but in Liptak’s view, it amounted to “a gift” to the Chief and his fellow justices, since it “inadvertently made the case for the Court’s independence.”

In terms of his official title, Chief Justice Roberts is actually the chief justice not of the Supreme Court, but of the United States. As the leader of the entire federal judiciary, his job includes protecting judges from unwarranted hostility and threats—which are sadly on the rise, as five lower-court judges discussed at a virtual event.

During a Tuesday event at Rice University, Judge Lee Rosenthal (S.D. Tex.) asked Chief Justice Roberts how he deals with criticism. After noting that it comes with the job if you’re a judge, he added that sometimes “the criticism can move from the focus on legal analysis to personalities.” Such “personally directed hostility,” according to the Chief, “is dangerous—and it’s got to stop.” Chief Justice Roberts’s remarks generated extensive media coverage, collected at How Appealing by Howard Bashman (along with video—the key discussion begins around the 23-minute mark).

The Supreme Court has received criticism for not being sufficiently supportive of lower-court judges, especially when they rule against the Trump administration. So I think the Chief’s remarks—which didn’t call out Trump by name, but clearly implicate him and his supporters—are noteworthy. As Judge Rosenthal put it, “On behalf of trial judges everywhere, I want to personally thank you because while we know that you may not always agree with us, we always know that you have our backs, and that means a great deal. So, thank you—and I hope it continues.”

In other news about judges and the judiciary:

Sorry, he’s not sorry: in a speech delivered after his controversial “swinging dicks” dissent, Judge Lawrence VanDyke argued that his Ninth Circuit colleagues are the ones who should apologize, for “routinely impos[ing] their personal policy preferences on the law.” He then offered them a deal: “You stop breaking the norm of how our system was designed to work, and I’ll stop breaking those norms that promote collegiality and the perception of legitimacy.” For anyone who isn’t tired of this story—i.e., can’t get enough “dick,” à la Blanche Devereaux of The Golden Girls—check out Sarah Isgur and David French’s discussion on Advisory Opinions. (And for anyone who skimmed my story about that dissent too quickly, please note that this sentence was a joke: “Not to be outdone, Judge James Ho subsequently issued a Fifth Circuit opinion that opened as follows: ‘This is a case about huge, throbbing cocks.’”)

Judge Zahid Quraishi (D.N.J.) is quite upset over the leadership structure at my former workplace, the U.S. Attorney’s Office in New Jersey, currently being run by a “triumvirate” after the departure of Alina Habba—and he took it out on two assistant U.S. attorneys, Daniel Rosenblum and Mark Coyne, who appeared before him for a sentencing. After reading the transcript, my own view is that Judge Quraishi was unnecessarily harsh toward the two AUSAs, who didn’t come up with this (admittedly bizarre) scheme but are stuck defending it. (Disclosure: I’m not unbiased, since Mark Coyne is a friend and former colleague of mine.)

In nominations news, the Senate confirmed Anna St. John to serve as a judge on the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Louisiana, by a vote of 51-45. St. John currently serves as president and general counsel of the Hamilton Lincoln Law Institute, a nonprofit public-interest firm (mentioned in last week’s Judicial Notice for filing a proposed class action on behalf of a putative class of drivers who got stuck for hours in a massive traffic jam caused by a pro-Palestinian protest).

In memoriam: Judge E. Grady Jolly, who served on the Fifth Circuit for more than 40 years, passed away at 88. For personal reflections on Judge Jolly from a former clerk, see Ilya Shapiro’s tribute over at City Journal.

Job of the Week: an opportunity for an appellate litigator in Houston.

Lateral Link is working exclusively with the leader of a Chambers-ranked appellate group in Houston seeking a strong associate to join its team. This is a rare opportunity to join a highly respected, close-knit practice where associates take on meaningful responsibility early and work directly with partners on complex, high-level matters. The group is known for its collegial, mentorship-driven culture, offering a clear path to partnership. They seek an associate with two to seven years of experience, a federal trial- or appellate-court clerkship, outstanding academic credentials, and excellent research and writing skills. If you’re interested—or even just curious—please contact Wendy Boone at wendyboone@laterallink.com.

Rulings of the Week: Ford v. McKesson and United States v. Burger.

Last week, I highlighted a trio of Ninth Circuit rulings in First Amendment cases that generally came down on the side of free speech (and please note the update I appended to my discussion of NetChoice, LLC v. Bonta). But this week, I’m featuring a pair of Fifth Circuit rulings, issued on the same day, in which the First Amendment claims did not prevail:

Ford v. McKesson. In this long-running litigation filed by John Ford, a Baton Rouge police officer, against DeRay McKesson, a Black Lives Matter activist, a divided panel of the Fifth Circuit ruled that after eight years of legal sparring, “[i]t is time for Officer Ford to have a jury assess his claim that DeRay Mckesson’s negligence in leading a violent protest caused him to suffer injuries at the hands of rioters.” Judge Edith Jones wrote the majority opinion; Judge Carolyn Dineen King dissented, arguing that the decision “imperils First Amendment liberties.”

United States v. Burger. In January 2025, a federal grand jury indicted James Wesley Burger, an 18-year-old high school senior at the time, on three counts of transmitting threats in interstate commerce. But he didn’t make these alleged threats in person or over the phone; instead, he made them to other users of the game Roblox. The district court dismissed the indictment, concluding that no reasonable juror could find that Burger’s statements were “true threats” outside the protection of the First Amendment. But in a unanimous, per curiam opinion, the Fifth Circuit reversed, holding that “a trial on the merits is necessary to evaluate the sufficiency of the government’s evidence.”

The Fifth Circuit is the most conservative circuit court in the country, and this sometimes manifests itself in decisions that could be construed as against free speech (although note that some of the court’s conservative members, including Judges Jim Ho and Don Willett, are some of the strongest defenders of the First Amendment). I also think it’s fair to say that Ford and Burger might have come out differently if they had been litigated in another, more liberal circuit court.

But note a common theme tying together Ford and Burger: they could be viewed less as cases against free speech and more as cases in favor of letting juries decide. In these two litigations, context matters—the context of the protest, the context of the video game—and jurors might be better situated than judges to make the required calls. So let’s see how the trials turn out before fearing for the First Amendment.

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

State of New York v. Trump. From John Ross of Short Circuit: “Within days of President Trump beginning his second term, OMB orders a sweeping freeze to all federal financial assistance programs. The following day, 22 states sue, alleging that the freeze is unlawful. The following day, OMB rescinds the memo announcing the policy. Feds: So the lawsuit is moot. First Circuit [by Chief Judge David Barron]: Then why did the White House Press Secretary post, ‘This is NOT a rescission of the federal funding freeze. It is simply a rescission of the OMB memo’? The case is live and the preliminary injunction against the freeze is affirmed. But the part of the injunction actually requiring disbursement of the money is vacated.”

In terms of Trump-related litigation in the district courts:

Judge Brian Murphy (D. Mass.) blocked the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) from implementing certain vaccine-related policies promulgated by Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.

Speaking of Secretary Kennedy, Judge Mustafa Kasubhai (D. Or.) blocked HHS’s effort to restrict healthcare providers from providing gender-affirming care to minors.

Judge Paul L. Friedman (D.D.C.) held unconstitutional the Trump administration’s new restrictions on media reporters and news organizations covering the Pentagon—and noted that “in light of the country’s recent incursion into Venezuela and its ongoing war with Iran, it is more important than ever that the public have access to information from a variety of perspectives about what its government is doing.”

Staying on the media beat, Judge Royce Lamberth (D.D.C.) ruled that the administration’s near shutdown of Voice of America was illegal—and ordered the reinstatement of more than 1,000 people who were placed on leave at the government-funded broadcaster.

On Friday, a federal jury in San Francisco (N.D. Cal.) found Elon Musk liable for some losses experienced by Twitter investors, based on comments he posted on social media threatening to walk away from his purchase of the company. Damages remain to be determined, but lawyers for the plaintiff investors claim that Musk might have to pay former Twitter shareholders up to $2.5 billion.

Whenever you see a giant jury verdict, take it with a grain of salt, at least until all post-trial motions and appeals have been resolved. Los Angeles County Superior Court Judge Ruth Kwan granted Johnson & Johnson’s motion to set aside an award of $950 million in punitive damages to the family of a woman who died from mesothelioma, which they claimed was caused by J&J’s talc products.

Turning to the state courts, my favorite verdict of last week came in Shawn D. Cooley v. John Edgar Foreman aka Afroman. In 2022, police officers in southern Ohio raided Afroman’s home, investigating (false) tips of kidnapping and drug trafficking. The police found no evidence of such crimes and criminal charges were never brought, but the officers did cause significant damage to Afroman’s property. He did what you’d expect a rapper to do: he made multiple (humorous) songs about the raid, using real video from the raid (caught on his home security cameras), and he mocked the officers mercilessly, calling them out by name. They sued him, claiming defamation and privacy violations. But they had a weak case, as explained by Aaron Terr of the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE), and a jury ruled entirely in Afroman’s favor last Wednesday. For more on the case, read this tweet, watch this video, or read this Times article.

But whether states can be as effective as the feds in this area remains to be seen.

Law Firm of the Week: Kirkland & Ellis.

Thanks in large part to its work advising on billion-dollar deals like Nexstar/Tegna, Kirkland & Ellis set another record. Based on its 2025 financial results, K&E became the first law firm in history to hit more than $10 billion in annual revenue—and its equity partners earned more than $11 million in profits per equity partner.

What’s prompting Lefkowitz’s departure? Here’s one possibility: in 2007, he was part of a legal team that represented Jeffrey Epstein, obtaining what was widely viewed as a sweetheart deal for some of the late financier’s sex crimes. And the two men interacted outside the context of Lefkowitz’s legal representation: as reported by The Financial Times, Lefkowitz invited Epstein to his son’s bar mitzvah, and Lefkowitz also asked to borrow Epstein’s helicopter for a trip to the Hamptons.

When asked by Patrick Smith of The American Lawyer about whether his retirement from Kirkland might be related to his work for Epstein, Lefkowitz responded with a statement: “In 2007, I represented Epstein, like every one of my clients, as effectively as I could. Although I believe everyone is entitled to representation no matter how heinous their crimes, knowing what we all know now, I would not have taken on the matter.” As for Kirkland, a firm spokesperson stated, “To say the least, we deeply regret the firm’s 2007 representation of Jeffrey Epstein. It was clearly a mistake to take that on and, without question, we would not have made the same decision today.”

In other news of departures from K&E, partners are starting to follow David Nemecek, a leading figure in the liability-management space, over to his new home, Simpson Thacher. They include restructuring partners Brian Schartz and John Luze, as reported by The WSJ, and restructuring partner Christine Bae and finance partner Jacob Ruby, per Law.com.

Move of the Week: Manatt hiring entertainment lawyers Ellie Heisler, Jessica Boardman, and Mike Lawhead.

Since the Oscars just took place, it seems fitting to feature showbiz lawyers as the stars of the latest Move of the Week. As reported by Variety and Law360, three entertainment partners—Ellie Heisler, Jessica Boardman, and Mike Lawhead—joined the Los Angeles office of Manatt Phelps & Phillips. Heisler, Lawhead (great name for an attorney), and associate April Hua joined from Nixon Peabody; Boardman came from an entertainment-focused boutique, Myman Greenspan.

Other notable moves:

Not too far away (depending on L.A. traffic), Goodwin Procter opened an Orange County office in Newport Beach. The O.C. outpost will be led by Richard Grabowski, who left Jones Day after 36 years, along with two other litigation partners, Ryan Ball and John Vogt.

Speaking of new offices, Reed Smith launched in Boston, its fourth new outpost in 14 months, with a dozen lawyers from seven different Am Law 50 firms—including Grant Butler, a Chambers-ranked regulatory lawyer focused on the financial industry, who came from K&L Gates.

Prominent trial lawyer Hildy Sastre moved to the Miami office of DLA Piper from Shook Hardy & Bacon, where she had co-chaired the product-liability practice.

Teal Luthy Miller, former acting U.S. attorney for the Western District of Washington, joined the Seattle office of Davis Wright Tremaine. (Fun fact: she’s married to Judge Eric Miller of the Ninth Circuit, whom she met when they were classmates at the University of Chicago Law School.)

Abbe Lowell, who has been extremely busy with his many Trump-related matters, hired his first partner at Lowell & Associates: former federal prosecutor Caleb Hayes-Deats, who came from MoloLamken.

Thanks for joining me for this edition of Judicial Notice. I’m glad to be back in the swing of things, after a lovely vacation, and I wish you all the best in the week to come.
